text.content:
Impulse is a sudden force applied over an instant — a kick, a punch, a collision. Unlike continuous forces like gravity, an impulse transfers momentum in a single frame. When our stick figure's foot connects with the ball, we apply an impulse vector that encodes both direction and magnitude.

Momentum is conserved in every collision. The ball's post-kick velocity depends on the impulse force, the ball's mass, and its coefficient of restitution (bounciness). A beach ball with high restitution bounces energetically; a bowling ball with low restitution would barely move.

Watch the ball fly through the text — this is the 'scatter' text mode. As the ball passes through the text area, words are displaced outward from the impact point. The displacement force decreases with distance, creating a ripple effect that makes the text feel like a physical medium rather than static content.

The running animation you see is driven entirely by motor blend targets. Each step alternates between two pose presets — running0 and running1 — cycling leg and arm positions to simulate a stride. The motors track these target angles with configurable stiffness, creating a convincing run cycle from simple joint rotations.

This demonstrates how complex-looking animations can emerge from simple rules. No keyframes, no tweening libraries — just physics motors tracking target poses, and the simulation handles all the in-between states naturally.
